title: Feelings & Health
focus: Name emotions and body parts, say "I need…," visit the nurse, basic health.
function: Say how I feel and what I need
foundationalLiteracy: Body-part labeling with a diagram. The -ache compound pattern (headache, stomachache, toothache) as a spelling and meaning family.
culturalNote: Handle this unit with care. Newcomers may have experienced trauma, injury, or loss in transit, and questions about the body and how you feel can surface it. Never require a student to disclose. Keep the emotional vocabulary factual and low-stakes, offer a non-verbal way to opt out, and know your school's referral path before you teach it. Also: describing pain to an adult is culturally variable — some students are taught to endure it silently. Say plainly that telling the nurse is the right thing to do here.

1.canDo: Point to a body part or a feeling face when it is named.
1.scaffold: Body diagram and a feelings-face chart. Pointing is a complete answer.
1.check: Identifies 6 body parts and 4 feelings from spoken words.

2.canDo: Understand a spoken question about how I feel and what hurts.
2.scaffold: Nurse role-play script with picture support. Questions limited to 4 known frames.
2.check: Responds appropriately to 4 of 5 spoken health questions.

3.canDo: Follow spoken health instructions and understand a follow-up question.
3.scaffold: Recorded nurse dialogue at normal rate with a note-catcher.
3.check: Reports the instruction given and answers a follow-up about duration or severity.

1.canDo: Say one word or point to show how I feel or what hurts.
1.scaffold: Feelings card and body diagram on the desk. Pointing accepted without speech.
1.check: Communicates a need or feeling to an adult by word or gesture.

2.canDo: Tell the nurse what hurts and how I feel using a frame.
2.scaffold: Frame strip: My ___ hurts. I feel ___. I need ___.
2.check: Produces a 2-sentence report of a symptom and a need.

3.canDo: Describe a symptom with detail and answer follow-up questions.
3.scaffold: Detail bank: since when, how bad, what helps. Open frame.
3.check: Describes a symptom with onset and severity and answers two follow-ups.

1.canDo: Recognize health and safety signs and the word nurse.
1.scaffold: Real signage photos: nurse, exit, first aid, do not enter.
1.check: Matches 5 safety signs to their meanings.

2.canDo: Read a simple health form and find the fields I must fill in.
2.scaffold: Enlarged form with required fields highlighted and icons on each label.
2.check: Identifies all required fields on an unfamiliar simple form.

3.canDo: Read a short set of care instructions and say what to do and when.
3.scaffold: Instruction card with a sequencing organizer (first / then / if).
3.check: Orders 4 instruction steps and identifies the condition that triggers step 4.

1.canDo: Circle or copy a feeling word on a check-in slip.
1.scaffold: Daily check-in slip with four faces and words. Circling is sufficient.
1.check: Completes a daily check-in independently for five consecutive days.

2.canDo: Fill in a nurse pass or a simple health form.
2.scaffold: Labeled form, word bank of symptoms and body parts.
2.check: Completes a nurse pass with name, symptom, and time.

3.canDo: Write a short note explaining an absence or a health need.
3.scaffold: Model note plus a reason bank. Requires a date and a reason.
3.check: Writes a 3-sentence note with date, reason, and request.

workedTask.prompt: Tell someone you don't feel well

1.scaffold: Points to the place that hurts on their own body or on a diagram and says the single word. No sentence required — the goal is that a hurt student gets help.

2.say: I feel [feeling]. My [body part] hurts. I need [need].
2.scaffold: Three-slot frame on a card the student can hand to the nurse if speaking is too hard in the moment.

3.say: I've had a [symptom] since [time]. It hurts when I [action]. I think I need to [request].
3.scaffold: Open frame plus a detail bank for onset and severity — the language a doctor's office will expect later in life.
